Generation Z and Mental Health

Updated May 12, 2024 | Posted March 3, 2021
By the Annie E. Casey Foundation

MemôÙbers of GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z ã defined here as indiôÙvidôÙuôÙals born between 1997 and 2012 ã are growôÙing up in an age of increased stress and anxôÙiôÙety. Some 84% of Gen Zers believe menôÙtal health is a criôÙsis in the UnitôÙed States, and they are over 80% more likeôÙly to report dealôÙing with anxôÙiôÙety or depresôÙsion comôÙpared to oldôÙer genôÙerôÙaôÙtions, accordôÙing to recent Gen Z studies.

Why Focus on MenôÙtal Health for Gen Z, Specifically?

ForôÙmer U.S. SurôÙgeon GenôÙerôÙal David SatchôÙer once said, ããThere is no health withôÙout menôÙtal health,ã which is true for all age groups. Gen Zers, rangôÙing from ages 12 to 27 in 2024, are unique because the vast majorôÙiôÙty are going through a critôÙiôÙcal stage of develôÙopôÙment. A large body of research has shown that the age span of roughôÙly 14 through 24 marks a forôÙmaôÙtive phase of life in which proôÙfound cogôÙniôÙtive, bioôÙlogôÙiôÙcal and psyôÙchosoôÙcial changes are takôÙing place. At the same time, these young peoôÙple must navôÙiôÙgate increasôÙing autonôÙoôÙmy, formôÙing their idenôÙtiôÙties, develôÙopôÙing relaôÙtionôÙship and life skills, obtainôÙing eduôÙcaôÙtion and career trainôÙing and more. This is also a vulôÙnerôÙaôÙble time for adoôÙlesôÙcent menôÙtal health, as about 75% of menôÙtal illôÙnessôÙes emerge between ages 10 and 24. This critôÙiôÙcal periôÙod is an imporôÙtant winôÙdow of opporôÙtuôÙniôÙty to supôÙport young peoôÙple, proôÙmote their menôÙtal well-being and help set them on a posôÙiôÙtive path for the future.

Gen Zers are also corôÙrect in their assessmentãthey are facôÙing a menôÙtal health criôÙsis. LeadôÙing orgaôÙniôÙzaôÙtions such as the AmerôÙiôÙcan AcadôÙeôÙmy of PediôÙatrics and the AmerôÙiôÙcan AcadôÙeôÙmy of Child and AdoôÙlesôÙcent PsyôÙchiôÙaôÙtry recentôÙly declared a nationôÙal state of emerôÙgency for child and youth menôÙtal health. And while most young peoôÙple are physôÙiôÙcalôÙly and emoôÙtionôÙalôÙly healthy, trends in youth suiôÙcide, menôÙtal health ER visôÙits, depresôÙsion and anxôÙiôÙety have been on the rise over the past decade or more. This criôÙsis has affectôÙed younger chilôÙdren and milôÙlenôÙniôÙals, too. GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z has been excepôÙtionôÙalôÙly open about sharôÙing their strugôÙgles, as described in this post.

Note: When conôÙsidôÙerôÙing the menôÙtal health of GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z, it can be useôÙful to rememôÙber that:

  • Gen Z is not a monoôÙlith. Theyãre extremeôÙly diverse ã racialôÙly, linôÙguisôÙtiôÙcalôÙly, culôÙturôÙalôÙly and socioeôÙcoôÙnomôÙiôÙcalôÙly ã and more likeôÙly to idenôÙtiôÙfy as LGBTQ+ than oldôÙer age groups. NatôÙuôÙralôÙly, they have many influôÙences on their menôÙtal health beyond the genôÙerôÙaôÙtion in which they were born.
  • ComôÙparôÙisons across genôÙerôÙaôÙtions are tricky because it is difôÙfiôÙcult to tease apart what might be actuôÙal genôÙerôÙaôÙtional difôÙferôÙences from demoôÙgraphôÙic popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion shifts or age-relatôÙed facôÙtors, such as develôÙopôÙmenôÙtal phasôÙes and difôÙferôÙences in health care access.

GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z BatôÙtles AnxôÙiôÙety and Depression

NearôÙly two-thirds (65%) of Gen Zers reportôÙed expeôÙriôÙencôÙing at least one menôÙtal health probôÙlem in the past two years, accordôÙing to a mulôÙti-year study released in 2023. This staôÙtisôÙtic was lowôÙer for all oldôÙer genôÙerôÙaôÙtions, includôÙing milôÙlenôÙniôÙals (51%), Gen Xers (29%) and Boomers (14%).

While these difôÙferôÙences may be parôÙtialôÙly explained by Gen Zãs stage of life, some research indiôÙcates that Gen Z has highôÙer rates of self-reportôÙed menôÙtal health chalôÙlenges comôÙpared to preôÙviôÙous genôÙerôÙaôÙtions at the same age. For examôÙple, the latôÙest CDC Youth Risk BehavôÙior SurôÙvey data shows that 42% of Gen Z high schoolôÙers reportôÙed perôÙsisôÙtent feelôÙings of sadôÙness or hopeôÙlessôÙness in 2021, which is nearôÙly 50% highôÙer than reports of milôÙlenôÙniôÙal high schoolôÙers in the earôÙly 2000s. Among girls, this figôÙure was 35% for milôÙlenôÙniôÙal high schoolôÙers in 2001 comôÙpared to 57% of Gen Z high schoolôÙers in 2021.

Recent surôÙveys of Gen Zers show wideôÙspread self-reportôÙed strugôÙgles with anxôÙiôÙety and depresôÙsion. For instance:

  • A 2023 Gallup surôÙvey found that almost half (47%) of Gen Zers ages 12 to 26 often or always feel anxôÙious, and more than one in five (22%) often or always feel depressed. FigôÙures were even highôÙer for the females and adults in this samôÙple, and they were alarmôÙingôÙly high for LGBTQ+ adults (with 74% feelôÙing anxôÙious and 50% feelôÙing depressed).
  • Among Gen Z young adults ages 18 to 24, a fall 2022 CenôÙsus Bureau surôÙvey found that more than two in five (44%) reportôÙed perôÙsisôÙtent nerôÙvous, on edge or anxôÙious feelôÙings, and one in three (33%) reportôÙed perôÙsisôÙtent depressed, down or hopeôÙless feelôÙings. These surôÙvey quesôÙtions are well-estabôÙlished screenôÙers for depresôÙsion and anxôÙiôÙety disorders.
  • AccordôÙing to a 2022 fedôÙerôÙal surôÙvey of nearôÙly 15,000 Gen Z youth ages 12 to 17, one in five (20%) had a major depresôÙsive episode in the preôÙviôÙous year, equivôÙaôÙlent to 4.8 milôÙlion adoôÙlesôÙcents. An even greater shareã25%ãhad either a major depresôÙsive episode or a subôÙstance use disôÙorôÙder in the past year. 
  • The same fedôÙerôÙal surôÙvey asked difôÙferôÙent menôÙtal health quesôÙtions of peoôÙple 18 and oldôÙer, but simôÙiôÙlarôÙly found that 20% of Gen Z young adults ages 18 to 25 also expeôÙriôÙenced a major depresôÙsive episode in the past year. This figôÙure tends to be lowôÙer for oldôÙer age groups: 10% for ages 26 to 49 and 5% for those 50 and above in 2022.
  • FurôÙther, accordôÙing to this fedôÙerôÙal surôÙvey, more than one in three (36%) Gen Z young adults ages 18 to 25 had ããAny MenôÙtal IllôÙnessã in the past year, which means any menôÙtal, behavôÙioral, or emoôÙtionôÙal disôÙorôÙder of sufôÙfiôÙcient length to meet clinôÙiôÙcal diagôÙnosôÙtic criôÙteôÙria, excludôÙing develôÙopôÙmenôÙtal and subôÙstance use disôÙorôÙders. This is equivôÙaôÙlent to 12.6 milôÙlion young people.

MenôÙtal Health ER VisôÙits and HosôÙpiôÙtalôÙizaôÙtions BackôÙup Gen Z Self-Reports

In addiôÙtion to GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Zãs self-reports of depresôÙsion, anxôÙiôÙety and othôÙer strugôÙgles, data on menôÙtal health hosôÙpiôÙtalôÙizaôÙtions and emerôÙgency room (ER) visôÙits demonôÙstrate simôÙiôÙlarôÙly conôÙcernôÙing findings.

  • Among young peoôÙple ages 6 to 24, the share of ER visôÙits for menôÙtal health probôÙlems nearôÙly douôÙbled from 2011 to 2020ãand suiôÙcide-relatôÙed visôÙits increased five-foldãaccording to a 2023 study. (Gen Z spanned ages 8 to 23 in 2020.) These increasôÙes occurred for younger chilôÙdren, adoôÙlesôÙcents, and young adults, with the largest jump observed for adoôÙlesôÙcents. MenôÙtal health-relatôÙed ER visôÙits increased sigôÙnifôÙiôÙcantôÙly across all demoôÙgraphôÙic groups studôÙied, e.g., by genôÙder, race and ethôÙnicôÙiôÙty, insurôÙance type and geography.
  • AddiôÙtionôÙal research found that ER visôÙits for suiôÙcide attempts conôÙtinôÙued to climb in 2021 for youth ages 12 to 17, and that menôÙtal health-relatôÙed ER visôÙits among young peoôÙple are genôÙerôÙalôÙly becomôÙing more comôÙplex and resultôÙing in longer hosôÙpiôÙtal stays.
  • Among all youth ages 11 to 20 who are hosôÙpiôÙtalôÙized in the UnitôÙed States, more than one in five have a menôÙtal health or subôÙstance use diagôÙnoôÙsis, accordôÙing to the AmerôÙiôÙcan AcadôÙeôÙmy of Pediatrics. 
  • Data show risôÙing menôÙtal health hosôÙpiôÙtalôÙizaôÙtions and trouôÙbling readôÙmisôÙsion rates among chilôÙdren and youth in the past decade. For instance, a 2023 study found that such hosôÙpiôÙtalôÙizaôÙtions increased by more than 25% between 2009 and 2019, and hosôÙpiôÙtalôÙizaôÙtions due to suiôÙcide or self-injury rose at least 1.6ãfold.

Why Is GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z So Depressed?

There is no simôÙple or definôÙiôÙtive answer, but experts sugôÙgest a range of posôÙsiôÙble conôÙtribôÙuôÙtors to the rise in menôÙtal health probôÙlems among young peoôÙple, includôÙing but not limôÙitôÙed to:

  • High rates of social media use
  • WorsôÙenôÙing stress and social conôÙtexts due to issues like cliôÙmate change, mass shootôÙings, racial vioôÙlence and the opiôÙoid epidemic
  • Long-term effects of ecoôÙnomôÙic inequities

Recent surôÙveys have asked Gen Zers what they think is conôÙtributôÙing to their menôÙtal health chalôÙlenges. Hereãs what they said:

FinanôÙcial stress and achieveôÙment presôÙsure: A majorôÙiôÙty (56%) of Gen Z young adults ages 18 to 25 say finanôÙcial worôÙries are negôÙaôÙtiveôÙly influôÙencôÙing their menôÙtal health, and half (51%) say achieveôÙment presôÙsure is havôÙing the same impact, accordôÙing to a 2022 nationôÙalôÙly repôÙreôÙsenôÙtaôÙtive surôÙvey by HarôÙvard UniôÙverôÙsiôÙty. In a simôÙiôÙlar vein, a 2023 Gallup surôÙvey found that more than two-thirds (69%) of Gen Zers ages 12 to 26 say their most imporôÙtant hope for the future is to earn enough monôÙey to be comôÙfortôÙable, yet 64% see finanôÙcial resources as a barôÙriôÙer to achievôÙing their goals or aspirationsãby far the top reportôÙed barrier.

Lack of life direcôÙtion and purôÙpose: The same HarôÙvard study found that half of Gen Z young adults say their menôÙtal health is negôÙaôÙtiveôÙly affectôÙed by not knowôÙing what to do with their lives, and almost three in five (58%) lacked meanôÙing or purôÙpose in their lives withôÙin the past month. Among young peoôÙple ages 12 to 26, anothôÙer 2023 Gallup surôÙvey found that the biggest driôÙver of Gen Z hapôÙpiôÙness is their sense of purôÙpose at either school or work, but 43% to 49% of Gen Zers ããdo not feel what they do each day is interôÙestôÙing, imporôÙtant or motivating.ã

CliôÙmate change and globôÙal worôÙries: NearôÙly half (45%) of young adults ages 18 to 25 think their menôÙtal health is harmed by an overôÙall ããsense that things are falling apart,ã and one in three (34%) say cliôÙmate change is havôÙing a negôÙaôÙtive effect. A recent interôÙnaôÙtionôÙal study of 10,000 young peoôÙple ages 16 to 25 also found that more than 80% were worôÙried about the cliôÙmate criôÙsis, with many expressôÙing feelôÙings of sadôÙness, anxôÙiôÙety, anger and powôÙerôÙlessôÙness.

A need for conôÙnecôÙtion with othôÙers: SadôÙly, more than two in five (44%) Gen Z young adults feel like they donãt matôÙter to othôÙers, and one in three (34%) report loneôÙliôÙness, accordôÙing to the same HarôÙvard surôÙvey. This is corôÙrobôÙoôÙratôÙed by 2023 Gallup findôÙings that about one in three Gen Zers ages 12 to 26 do not often feel loved (31%) or supôÙportôÙed (35%) by othôÙers, and a simôÙiôÙlar share (30%) always/ãoften feel like nobody knows them well. This is espeôÙcialôÙly trouôÙbling givôÙen that these young peoôÙple are going through a vulôÙnerôÙaôÙble develôÙopôÙmenôÙtal stage, and eviôÙdence indiôÙcates that staôÙble, supôÙportôÙive relaôÙtionôÙships are imporôÙtant for posôÙiôÙtive menôÙtal health.

Gun vioôÙlence and social issues in AmerôÙiôÙca: More than two in five (42%) Gen Z young adults say gun vioôÙlence negôÙaôÙtiveôÙly affects their menôÙtal health, and 70% see this as a pubôÙlic health issue. MulôÙtiôÙple surôÙveys also find that Gen Z is conôÙcerned about access to health care, reproôÙducôÙtive health care, racial jusôÙtice, LGBTQ+ rights, and othôÙer social issues.

TechôÙnolôÙoôÙgy likeôÙly plays a role, too. On one hand, growôÙing up in a hyperôÙconôÙnectôÙed world can proôÙvide access to posôÙiôÙtive social conôÙnecôÙtions and supôÙportôÙive resources. On the othôÙer hand, it can fuel a steady drumôÙbeat of negôÙaôÙtive news stoôÙries, encourôÙage unhealthy social comôÙparôÙisons and increase the risk of online harassôÙment. LGBTQ+ youth and young peoôÙple of colôÙor face parôÙticôÙuôÙlar risks of disôÙcrimôÙiôÙnaôÙtion on social media. Some research links highôÙ levôÙels of social media use among youth to adverse outôÙcomes, includôÙing depresôÙsion and inadôÙequate sleep. Those who are already strugôÙgling with menôÙtal health probôÙlems may be more likeôÙly to use social media, makôÙing it difôÙfiôÙcult to untanôÙgle the effects of technology.

What causôÙes depresôÙsion in young peoôÙple, generally?

MenôÙtal health conôÙdiôÙtions like depresôÙsion and anxôÙiôÙety disôÙorôÙders are comôÙplex and not caused by a sinôÙgle issue. Instead, mulôÙtiôÙple facôÙtors are involved and may range from brain chemôÙistry disôÙrupôÙtions and genetôÙic vulôÙnerôÙaôÙbilôÙiôÙty to trauôÙmatôÙic expeôÙriôÙences (e.g., abuse, expoôÙsure to vioôÙlence, death of a loved one) and negôÙaôÙtive thought patôÙterns. Many othôÙer facôÙtors and cirôÙcumôÙstances can increase the risk of these conôÙdiôÙtions in young peoôÙple, such as: subôÙstance abuse; chronôÙic physôÙiôÙcal health conôÙdiôÙtions; low self-esteem; LGBTQ+ youth who lack supôÙport; and long-term relaôÙtionôÙship or school probôÙlems. The conôÙtribôÙuôÙtors to depresôÙsion and othôÙer menôÙtal illôÙnessôÙes are difôÙferôÙent for every young perôÙson, and each indiôÙvidôÙual is influôÙenced by their unique bioôÙlogôÙiôÙcal, famôÙiôÙly, comôÙmuôÙniôÙty, culôÙturôÙal and enviôÙronôÙmenôÙtal contexts.

GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z and Suicide

The U.S. suiôÙcide rate for young peoôÙple ages 10 to 24 surged by 57% from 2009 to 2019. The rate conôÙtinôÙued climbôÙing through 2021 before declinôÙing in 2022. SuiôÙcide was the third leadôÙing cause of death for GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z overôÙall in 2022, who spanned ages 10 to 25 that year, although it was the secôÙond leadôÙing cause of death among younger (ages 10 to 14) and oldôÙer (20 to 24) Gen Zers. 

Data shows that suiôÙcide rates vary by demoôÙgraphôÙic facôÙtors, such as age, genôÙder, LGBTQ+ staôÙtus and race or ethnicity.

Girls and young women are more likeôÙly to plan and attempt suiôÙcide, but boys and young men are more likeôÙly to die by suiôÙcide per the CDCãs Youth Risk BehavôÙior SurôÙvey and LeadôÙing CausôÙes of Death. In 2022, for instance, males accountôÙed for 78% of suiôÙcides among youth ages 10 to 24. Researchers explain that males typôÙiôÙcalôÙly use more lethal means when attemptôÙing suicide.

AmerôÙiôÙcan IndiôÙan or AlasôÙka Native youth in the same age range have the highôÙest suiôÙcide rate comôÙpared to othôÙer racial/ãethnic groups, and Native HawaiôÙian or OthôÙer PacifôÙic Islander youth (not comôÙbined with Asian) have the secôÙond highôÙest rate. While rates are lowôÙer for Asian youth ages 10 to 24, suiôÙcide is the leadôÙing cause of death for this group, and alarmôÙing trends have been observed in recent decades, includôÙing suiôÙcide rates spikôÙing by 140% from 1998 to 2018 among Asian young peoôÙple. Rates are also lowôÙer for Black youth, although it is still the third leadôÙing cause of death for this group, and leadôÙers have been callôÙing attenôÙtion to conôÙcernôÙing increasôÙes here, as well. For examôÙple, a 2024 study notôÙed that the suiôÙcide rate for Black youth ages 10 to 24 jumped by 37% between 2018 and 2021, the largest increase of all racial and ethôÙnic groups in this period.

In addiôÙtion, the latôÙest Youth Risk BehavôÙior SurôÙvey data show that more than one in five (22%) U.S. high schoolôÙers seriôÙousôÙly conôÙsidôÙered attemptôÙing suiôÙcide in the past year and a starôÙtling one in 10 (10%) actuôÙalôÙly attemptôÙed suiôÙcide in 2021. Among racial and ethôÙnic groups, the share attemptôÙing suiôÙcide was highôÙest for AmerôÙiôÙcan IndiôÙan or AlasôÙka Native (16%), Black (15%) and mulôÙtiraôÙcial (12%) stuôÙdents. Gen Zers of colôÙor disôÙproôÙporôÙtionôÙateôÙly face risk facôÙtors for menôÙtal health conôÙdiôÙtions, such as less access to treatôÙment in addiôÙtion to expoôÙsure to racism, poverôÙty, food inseôÙcuôÙriôÙty and othôÙer adverse childôÙhood expeôÙriôÙences.

Too many young peoôÙple expeôÙriôÙence added stress due to disôÙcrimôÙiôÙnaôÙtion based on race, ethôÙnicôÙiôÙty, sexôÙuôÙal oriôÙenôÙtaôÙtion and genôÙder idenôÙtiôÙty. LeadôÙing health orgaôÙniôÙzaôÙtions, includôÙing the CDC and AmerôÙiôÙcan AcadôÙeôÙmy of PediôÙatrics, view racism and disôÙcrimôÙiôÙnaôÙtion as a major pubôÙlic health conôÙcern, acknowlôÙedgôÙing its known effects on the menôÙtal health of young people. 

In 2021ã2022. approxôÙiôÙmateôÙly 1.7 milôÙlion youth ages 12 to 17 (7%) reportôÙedôÙly had been treatôÙed or judged unfairôÙly because of their race or ethôÙnicôÙiôÙty, and about 636,000 (3%) because of their sexôÙuôÙal oriôÙenôÙtaôÙtion or genôÙder idenôÙtiôÙty. FurôÙther, a 2023 nationôÙal surôÙvey revealed that 60% of LGBTQ+ young peoôÙple ages 13 to 24 expeôÙriôÙenced disôÙcrimôÙiôÙnaôÙtion in the past year due to their sexôÙuôÙal oriôÙenôÙtaôÙtion or genôÙder idenôÙtiôÙty, and about one in four (24%) were physôÙiôÙcalôÙly threatôÙened or hurt for the same reaôÙson. Those who were threatôÙened or harmed were much more likeôÙly to attempt suicide.

SuiôÙcide attempts are tragôÙiôÙcalôÙly high for LGBTQ+ high school stuôÙdents, as well, at 22% in 2021, comôÙpared to 6% for hetôÙeroôÙsexôÙuôÙal stuôÙdents. And nearôÙly half (45%) of U.S. LGBTQ+ high schoolôÙers seriôÙousôÙly conôÙsidôÙered attemptôÙing suiôÙcide in the past year, three times the rate (15%) of their hetôÙeroôÙsexôÙuôÙal peers. 

The vast majorôÙiôÙty of LGBTQ+ youth say that they want menôÙtal health care, but most of them (56%) are not able to get it, accordôÙing to the 2023 surôÙvey notôÙed above. The barôÙriôÙers they list illusôÙtrate that too many youth lack the supôÙport they need, includôÙing fears of disôÙcussing conôÙcerns (47%) getôÙting perôÙmisôÙsion to access care (41%) and not being takôÙen seriôÙousôÙly (40%), among othôÙer fears. OthôÙer comôÙmon barôÙriôÙers were not being able to afford it (38%) and being afraid it wouldnãt work (33%).

In addiôÙtion to LGBTQ+ youth and young peoôÙple of colôÙor, suiôÙcide rates are also high for those with disôÙabilôÙiôÙties, youth in rurôÙal areas, and those in the fosôÙter care or juveôÙnile jusôÙtice sysôÙtems. ImmiôÙgrant and low-income young peoôÙple face eleôÙvatôÙed risks for menôÙtal health chalôÙlenges, as well.

Read more about teen suiôÙcide trends in our post on LeadôÙing CausôÙes on Death in Teens, which includes data on suiôÙcides by firearm and resources on how to reduce teen deaths.

Gen Z Tends To Be Open About MenôÙtal Health and SeekôÙing Help, But Canãt Always Access Care

While GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z has been called the most depressed genôÙerôÙaôÙtion, memôÙbers of this group are more likeôÙly than their oldôÙer peers to seek out menôÙtal health counôÙselôÙing or therôÙaôÙpy. Some 39% of Gen Zers ã a highôÙer rate than any preôÙviôÙous genôÙerôÙaôÙtion ã report workôÙing with a menôÙtal health proôÙfesôÙsionôÙal in perôÙson or online.

Still, access to care remains a probôÙlem, as illusôÙtratôÙed above for LGBTQ+ young peoôÙple. AccordôÙing to MenôÙtal Health AmerôÙiôÙca: 60% of Gen Z youth ages 12 to 17 with major depresôÙsion do not receive treatôÙment. This is roughôÙly in line with othôÙer studôÙies showôÙing that around half of chilôÙdren and youth who need menôÙtal health care do not get it.

Access to care is a chalôÙlenge for Gen Z young adults, too. A 2022 Kaiser FamôÙiôÙly FounôÙdaôÙtion surôÙvey found that almost half (47%) of young adults ages 18 to 29 did not get menôÙtal health care in the past year when they thought they might need it, with cost citôÙed among the top barôÙriôÙers. OthôÙer comôÙmonôÙly reportôÙed barôÙriôÙers to getôÙting help include stigôÙma (being afraid or embarôÙrassed), feelôÙing too busy or havôÙing difôÙfiôÙculôÙty getôÙting time off work, chalôÙlenges findôÙing a provider, insurôÙance limôÙiôÙtaôÙtions and not knowôÙing how to find services. 

Even though stigôÙma conôÙtinôÙues to be a comôÙmon barôÙriôÙer to menôÙtal health treatôÙment, many experts credôÙit Gen Z for helpôÙing to reduce stigôÙma around menôÙtal health. One study estiôÙmatôÙed that memôÙbers of GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z are 20% more willôÙing to talk about their menôÙtal health than oldôÙer genôÙerôÙaôÙtions. This is critôÙiôÙcal, as more openôÙness about emoôÙtionôÙal health issues not only helps de-stigôÙmaôÙtize the subôÙject, but also creôÙates more awareôÙness and opporôÙtuôÙniôÙties to receive and/ãor proôÙvide support.

MenôÙtal Health and MenôÙtal Health Care for GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z PeoôÙple of Color

Youth of colôÙor are less likeôÙly to receive menôÙtal health care comôÙpared to white youth. Many issues conôÙtribute to this disôÙparôÙiôÙty, includôÙing stigôÙma, disôÙtrust in providers, a lack of culôÙturôÙalôÙly comôÙpeôÙtent providers or difôÙfiôÙculôÙty findôÙing providers. LargôÙer strucôÙturôÙal inequities also conôÙtribute to access probôÙlems, such as socioeôÙcoôÙnomôÙic, health insurôÙance and geoôÙgraphôÙic barriers.

MenôÙtal Health AmerôÙiôÙca and othôÙers have reportôÙed that Asian youth are the least likeôÙly to receive menôÙtal health treatôÙment comôÙpared to adoôÙlesôÙcents in othôÙer racial or ethôÙnic groups. Inequities in access to care and unmet needs have also been docôÙuôÙmentôÙed for Black, LatiôÙno, AmerôÙiôÙcan IndiôÙan or AlasôÙka Native and mulôÙtiraôÙcial young peoôÙple. LimôÙitôÙed access to menôÙtal health serôÙvices for youth of colôÙor eleôÙvates the risk of poor outôÙcomes, and it might explain lowôÙer rates of diagôÙnoôÙsis for some groups. 

In comôÙmuôÙniôÙties of colôÙor (and sociôÙety in genôÙerôÙal), menôÙtal illôÙness and menôÙtal health care are often stigôÙmaôÙtized. For examôÙple, research on Asian AmerôÙiôÙcan, Native HawaiôÙian and PacifôÙic Islander comôÙmuôÙniôÙties indiôÙcates that menôÙtal health can be very stigôÙmaôÙtized, which affects the likeôÙliôÙhood of seekôÙing proôÙfesôÙsionôÙal care. LanôÙguage and culôÙture play an imporôÙtant role in perôÙcepôÙtions and comôÙmuôÙniôÙcaôÙtion of menôÙtal health issues, as well. For examôÙple, Asian AmerôÙiôÙcans tend to have lowôÙer rates of menôÙtal health diagôÙnoôÙsis because their sympôÙtoms are more likeôÙly to manôÙiôÙfest as physôÙiôÙcal health probôÙlems, accordôÙing to research. And in some native lanôÙguages, words such as ããdepressedã and ããanxôÙiousã do not directôÙly transôÙlate, pointôÙing to the need for providers with shared culôÙturôÙal and linôÙguisôÙtic backgrounds.

NearôÙly 75% of U.S. menôÙtal health care providers are white, accordôÙing to the AnxôÙiôÙety and DepresôÙsion AssoôÙciôÙaôÙtion of AmerôÙiôÙca. Experts note that peoôÙple of colôÙor may be less likeôÙly to trust proôÙfesôÙsionôÙals who do not share their culôÙturôÙal backôÙgrounds, and they may not have conôÙfiôÙdence that such proôÙfesôÙsionôÙals will proôÙvide culôÙturôÙalôÙly responôÙsive care. AddiôÙtionôÙalôÙly, trustôÙing health care providers, in genôÙerôÙal, can be a chalôÙlenge for peoôÙple of colôÙor givôÙen our countryãs hisôÙtoôÙry of sysôÙtemic racism, slavôÙery and genoôÙcide, which has conôÙtributed to interôÙgenôÙerôÙaôÙtional psyôÙchoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal trauma.

ThereôÙfore, even when Gen Z youth of colôÙor opt to seek help, they may strugôÙgle to find a proôÙfesôÙsionôÙal they trust, who underôÙstands their backôÙground or lanôÙguage and who proôÙvides culôÙturôÙalôÙly responôÙsive serôÙvices. FurôÙther, they may be hard-pressed to find a proôÙfesôÙsionôÙal at all, recôÙogôÙnizôÙing the genôÙerôÙal shortôÙage of menôÙtal health providers and the limôÙitôÙed menôÙtal health serôÙvices for adoôÙlesôÙcents in comôÙmuôÙniôÙties of colôÙor. Providers tend to be limôÙitôÙed in rurôÙal and low-income areas, as well. These youth also may face barôÙriôÙers due to cost of care or health insurôÙance. MenôÙtal illôÙness freôÙquentôÙly goes untreatôÙed in comôÙmuôÙniôÙties of colôÙor due to highôÙer levôÙels of uninôÙsurôÙance, as reportôÙed by the AnxôÙiôÙety and DepresôÙsion AssoôÙciôÙaôÙtion of AmerôÙiôÙca.

COVID-19ãs Effect on GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Zãs MenôÙtal Health

While menôÙtal health trends for Gen Z were going in the wrong direcôÙtion before the onset of COVID-19 in 2020, the panôÙdemôÙic exacôÙerôÙbatôÙed emoôÙtionôÙal health chalôÙlenges for youth and had a sigôÙnifôÙiôÙcant adverse impact on the lives of GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z.

The panôÙdemôÙic radôÙiôÙcalôÙly changed their famôÙiôÙly, comôÙmuôÙniôÙty, eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal, social and employôÙment expeôÙriôÙences. It shiftôÙed learnôÙing online. CreôÙatôÙed social isoôÙlaôÙtion. Closed comôÙmuôÙniôÙty proôÙgrams. DestaôÙbiôÙlized the econôÙoôÙmy. Derailed employôÙment and colôÙlege for many. Robbed young peoôÙple of a parôÙent or loved one. And promptôÙed some oldôÙer sibôÙlings to jugôÙgle new roles as teachôÙers and careôÙgivers for their famôÙiôÙlies. Many effects of the panôÙdemôÙic conôÙtinôÙue today. 

School cloôÙsures were parôÙticôÙuôÙlarôÙly hard on young peoôÙple and famôÙiôÙlies, by causôÙing social isoôÙlaôÙtion, weakôÙenôÙing learnôÙing expeôÙriôÙences and removôÙing a critôÙiôÙcal safeôÙty net for famôÙiôÙlies. About two in five famôÙiôÙlies with chilôÙdren (includôÙing Gen Zers under 18) had difôÙfiôÙculôÙty payôÙing for basic houseôÙhold expensôÙes durôÙing the panôÙdemôÙic and at least half lost employôÙment income after the panôÙdemôÙic hit. Schools offer conôÙsisôÙtent meals, medôÙical screenôÙings and supôÙport serôÙvices for many chilôÙdren and youth. In some areas, schools are also the only source of menôÙtal health serôÙvices for young peoôÙple ã parôÙticôÙuôÙlarôÙly for youth who idenôÙtiôÙfy as LGBTQ+ and for indiôÙvidôÙuôÙals from low-income houseôÙholds and famôÙiôÙlies of colôÙor. LosôÙing this safeôÙty net durôÙing the panôÙdemôÙic sigôÙnifôÙiôÙcantôÙly added to the hardôÙship expeôÙriôÙenced by milôÙlions of chilôÙdren, youth and families.

Among Gen Z young adults, many had their colôÙlege plans, jobs and finances knocked off track. For instance, in OctoôÙber 2020, more than 40% of U.S. houseôÙholds reportôÙed that a prospecôÙtive colôÙlege stuôÙdent was canôÙceling plans to attend comôÙmuôÙniôÙty colôÙlege, accordôÙing to CenôÙsus Bureau data. ColôÙlege enrollôÙment rates have conôÙtinôÙued to decline since the panôÙdemôÙic. AddiôÙtionôÙal CenôÙsus Bureau data on the KIDS COUNT Data CenôÙter demonôÙstrate some of the employôÙment and finanôÙcial difôÙfiôÙculôÙties they faced, as well: 

  • In April-May 2020, 63% of young adults ages 18 to 24 reportôÙed that they or a houseôÙhold memôÙber lost employôÙment income since the panôÙdemôÙic began.
  • Just under one in 10 (9%) young adults said they someôÙtimes or often did not have enough food to eat priôÙor to the panôÙdemôÙic but as of April-May 2020, 14% said they did not have enough to eat in the past week. While this figôÙure flucôÙtuôÙatôÙed over the next two years, it was still 13% as of OctoôÙber-NovemôÙber 2022.
  • In April-May 2020, more than one in five (22%) young adults said they had litôÙtle or no conôÙfiôÙdence in their abilôÙiôÙty to make their next rent or mortôÙgage payôÙment on time. This figôÙure declined to 13% as of the latôÙest data availôÙable in March-May 2022. 

GivôÙen all of these chalôÙlenges, it may not be surôÙprisôÙing that menôÙtal health conôÙcerns climbed durôÙing the panôÙdemôÙic, by all accounts. NearôÙly two in five (37%) U.S. high schoolôÙers said they expeôÙriôÙenced poor menôÙtal health durôÙing the panôÙdemôÙic, and over half (55%) said they endured emoôÙtionôÙal abuse by someôÙone at home, accordôÙing to the CDC. Among parôÙents, almost half (47%) said the panôÙdemôÙic had a negôÙaôÙtive effect on their childãs menôÙtal health, based on a nationôÙal 2022 Kaiser FamôÙiôÙly FounôÙdaôÙtion (KFF) surôÙvey. FurôÙther, about sevôÙen in 10 parôÙents were conôÙcerned about the impact of panôÙdemôÙic-caused isoôÙlaôÙtion or loneôÙliôÙness on teens, and more than eight in 10 were worôÙried about depresôÙsion or anxôÙiôÙety among teens. Most parôÙents were also worôÙried about adoôÙlesôÙcent strugôÙgles with subôÙstance use, self-harm and eatôÙing disorders.

The same KFF surôÙvey found that Black and LatiôÙno parôÙents were more likeôÙly than white parôÙents to be ããvery worôÙriedã about many adoôÙlesôÙcent emoôÙtionôÙal health issues. For examôÙple, more LatiôÙno parôÙents were ããvery worôÙriedã about loneôÙliôÙness or isoôÙlaôÙtion due to the panôÙdemôÙic comôÙpared to white parôÙents: 45% vs 27%, respecôÙtiveôÙly. And Black parôÙents were more likeôÙly than white parôÙents to worôÙry about teen depresôÙsion: 53% vs. 39%. (The surôÙvey did not break out addiôÙtionôÙal racial or ethôÙnic groups.)

Experts note that the panôÙdemôÙic magôÙniôÙfied existôÙing health inequities in AmerôÙiôÙca, exposôÙing gaps in the menôÙtal health and health care sysôÙtems, with the fallôÙout disôÙproôÙporôÙtionôÙateôÙly impactôÙing low-income popôÙuôÙlaôÙtions and peoôÙple of colôÙor. HowôÙevôÙer, on the posôÙiôÙtive side, the panôÙdemôÙic genôÙerôÙalôÙly increased awareôÙness of menôÙtal health issues and expandôÙed telemedôÙiôÙcine opporôÙtuôÙniôÙties, which expandôÙed access to serôÙvices for those facôÙing geoôÙgraphôÙic or othôÙer barôÙriôÙers to care. Still, menôÙtal health care needs to be betôÙter inteôÙgratôÙed into priôÙmaôÙry health care, among many othôÙer steps to improve menôÙtal health outôÙcomes for young people.

Read more about how the panôÙdemôÙic disôÙruptôÙed the lives of chilôÙdren and famôÙiôÙlies in 2020.

TakôÙing Action to Address Gen Z MenôÙtal Health

MilôÙlions of Gen Zers have been strugôÙgling for too long, and inequities in menôÙtal health conôÙdiôÙtions and access to care must be improved. While youth menôÙtal health has gained pubôÙlic attenôÙtion and tracôÙtion in recent years, much more effort is needôÙed at the nationôÙal, state and local levels. 

The folôÙlowôÙing resources offer a range of strateôÙgies that can be takôÙen now by polôÙiôÙcyôÙmakôÙers, adminôÙisôÙtraôÙtors, funôÙders, eduôÙcaôÙtion leadôÙers and eduôÙcaôÙtors, varôÙiôÙous serôÙvice providers, advoôÙcates, famôÙiôÙlies, youth and othôÙers. At minôÙiôÙmum, it will require mulôÙtiôÙple secôÙtors: givôÙing this issue highôÙer priôÙorôÙiôÙty; backôÙing it up with adeôÙquate fundôÙing, polôÙiôÙcy and infraôÙstrucôÙture; strengthôÙenôÙing colôÙlabôÙoôÙraôÙtion across orgaôÙniôÙzaôÙtions and secôÙtors; increasôÙing menôÙtal health serôÙvices and supôÙports, espeôÙcialôÙly at schools; improvôÙing the diverôÙsiôÙty of and trainôÙing for the menôÙtal health workôÙforce; and focusôÙing on equiôÙty and youth empowôÙerôÙment at every step.

  • Youth MenôÙtal Health Report, NationôÙal Action Alliance for SuiôÙcide Prevention
  • 2024 NationôÙal StratôÙeôÙgy for SuiôÙcide PreôÙvenôÙtion and FedôÙerôÙal Action Plan, U.S. DepartôÙment of Health and Human Services
  • BlueôÙprint for Youth SuiôÙcide PreôÙvenôÙtion, AmerôÙiôÙcan AcadôÙeôÙmy of Pediatrics
  • StrengthôÙenôÙing Youth MenôÙtal Health: A Governorãs PlayôÙbook, NationôÙal GovôÙerôÙnors AssoôÙciôÙaôÙtion (also see MenôÙtal Health Americaãs sumôÙmaôÙry of the playôÙbook)
  • PartôÙnerôÙing with Schools to Improve Youth MenôÙtal Health, School-Based Health Alliance and NationôÙal CounôÙcil for MenôÙtal Wellbeing
  • ProôÙmotôÙing MenôÙtal Health and Well-Being in Schools: An Action Guide for School and DisôÙtrict LeadôÙers, CDC
  • MenôÙtal Health, SubôÙstance Use, and WellôÙbeôÙing in HighôÙer EduôÙcaôÙtion: SupôÙportôÙing the Whole StuôÙdent, NationôÙal AcadôÙeôÙmies of SciôÙences, EngiôÙneerôÙing, and Medicine
  • On Edge: UnderôÙstandôÙing and PreôÙventôÙing Young Adultsã MenôÙtal Health ChalôÙlenges, HarôÙvard GradôÙuôÙate School of EduôÙcaôÙtion, MakôÙing CarôÙing ComôÙmon Project

More Resources on GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z and MenôÙtal Health

Stay curôÙrent on issues impactôÙing youth and young adults by signôÙing up for one of our newsletôÙters today!

ConôÙtinôÙue learnôÙing about GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z, GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Alpha, and how these young peoôÙple will shape Americaãs future:

  • Core CharôÙacôÙterôÙisôÙtics of GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z
  • What the StaôÙtisôÙtics Say About GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z
  • Social Issues That MatôÙter to GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z
  • StaôÙtisôÙtics SnapôÙshot: GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Z and Education
  • What Is GenôÙerôÙaôÙtion Alpha?
  • Social MediôÙaãs ConôÙcernôÙing Effect on Teen MenôÙtal Health
  • MenôÙtal Health and Asian AmerôÙiôÙcan and Native HawaiôÙian and PacifôÙic Islander Youth
  • 2023 KIDS COUNT Data Book

KIDS COUNT Data CenôÙter indiôÙcaôÙtors on Gen Z and othôÙer generations:

  • PopôÙuôÙlaôÙtion size by Gen Alpha, Gen Z, milôÙlenôÙniôÙals, Gen X and baby boomers, by race and ethnicity 
  • PeoôÙple livôÙing in poverôÙty by generation
  • PeoôÙple livôÙing in poverôÙty by genôÙerôÙaôÙtion and race and ethnicity 
  • PeoôÙple livôÙing in low-income houseôÙholds by generation 
  • PeoôÙple livôÙing in low-income houseôÙholds by genôÙerôÙaôÙtion and race and ethnicity

AddiôÙtionôÙal Resources to SupôÙport Youth MenôÙtal Health

  • Joe RibôÙsam on How to Improve Youth BehavôÙioral Health Care: The Foundationãs direcôÙtor of child welôÙfare and juveôÙnile jusôÙtice polôÙiôÙcy describes what it takes to build an inteôÙgratôÙed behavôÙioral health care sysôÙtem that sets young peoôÙple up to thrive.
  • StrengthôÙenôÙing TribôÙal IdenôÙtiôÙty to PreôÙvent Youth SuiôÙcide: HighôÙlights a project showôÙing that, for young peoôÙple from hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcalôÙly underôÙrepôÙreôÙsentôÙed comôÙmuôÙniôÙties, includôÙing AmerôÙiôÙcan IndiôÙan and AlasôÙka Native youth, eviôÙôÙdence-based proôÙgrams that draw on culôÙturôÙal roots offer promisôÙing opporôÙtuôÙniôÙties to preôÙvent suicide. 
  • CreôÙatôÙing EquiôÙtable EcosysôÙtems of BelongôÙing and OpporôÙtuôÙniôÙty for Youth: DevelôÙoped with supôÙport from the FounôÙdaôÙtion, this guide aims to help leadôÙers build ecosysôÙtems that are betôÙter suitôÙed to ensure youth thrive. 
  • 988 SuiôÙcide & CriôÙsis LifeôÙline: Call, text or chat 24ã7 for free, conôÙfiôÙdenôÙtial supôÙport with trained criôÙsis counôÙselors. The nationôÙal 988 LifeôÙline proôÙvides preôÙvenôÙtion and criôÙsis resources if you or someôÙone you know is in distress.

This post is related to:

  • Generation Z
  • Health and Child Development
  • Youth Mental Health
